title-group.article-title: Alternatives to antibiotic growth promoters for poultry: a bibliometric analysis of the research journals

contrib-group.aff: ⁎Animal Bioscience and Biotechnology Laboratory, United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service, Beltsville, MD 20705, USA
†National Agricultural Library, United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service, Beltsville, MD 20705, USA
‡Office of National Program-Animal Health, Agricultural Research Service, US Department of Agriculture, Beltsville, MD 20705, USA

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).

abstract.p: The need to develop novel alternatives to antibiotics gained prominence following the ban on sub-therapeutic antibiotic applications for livestock growth enhancement. This prohibition led to a surge in research papers exploring potential alternatives to antibiotics to promote growth and health in poultry. As a result, it has become imperative to synthesize information regarding research accomplishments and publication patterns in antibiotic alternatives, to assess research gaps and aid regulatory, funding, and research entities in making informed decisions. Consequently, our study aims to systematically analyze and comprehend the research and publication trends related to growth-promoting antibiotic alternatives in poultry. We identified all publications during the search period from 2009 to 2022, utilizing various bibliometric analysis datasets from Scopus, Web of Science/InCites, and Dimensions. The Rayyan web application was employed for manual deduplicating, labeling, and screening the relevant publications. From an initial pool of 2038 publications, we screened and categorized 816 based on factors such as alternative antibiotic categories, publication years, countries, species, journals, and institutes. Our findings reveal that the most prevalent publications are centered around probiotic (30.51%) and phytogenic (24.02%). Notably, the United States leads publication output, followed by China and Egypt. Among poultry species, broilers emerge as the most extensively studied category, followed by layer chickens. Universities emerge as the foremost contributors to antibiotic alternative research, while government institutes and industry occupy the second and third positions, respectively. Upon scrutinizing the journals responsible for the highest publication count and most cited papers, it became evident that the journal “Poultry Science” leads with the highest percentage (13.51%) and the most highly cited publications, accounting for five out of eleven highly cited articles. This comprehensive review outlines research trends concerning diverse antibiotic alternatives, taking into account poultry species and geographical distribution. The future trajectory in this domain is projected to encompass a blend of various antibiotic alternatives that could be administered as a single product and/or the innovative use of novel antibiotic alternatives.

sec.title: INTRODUCTION
sec.p: With the projected rapid growth of the global population, which is now estimated to reach nearly 10 billion by 2050, food security is now one of the major public health concerns of the 21st century. In addition to basic food requirements for vulnerable populations, advances in economic and urban development and the associated rise in individual incomes have resulted in increased demand for better quality and healthier agriculture products. Accordingly, global food demand is estimated to increase by 56% to feed the growing world population by 2050 (van Dijk et al., 2021). According to recent estimations, the demand for meat products will double by 2050. This two-fold increase in demand for meat will be comprised of 181 million metric tons (mmt) for poultry, 143 mmt for pork, and 106 mmt for beef (Smith et al., 2015). To reach these meat production targets, farmers need to adopt highly intensified farming systems due to the decrease in available farmland. While the need for increasing animal production efficiency was previously supported by the use of antibiotic growth promoters (AGPs) that facilitated enhanced growth performance while minimizing common infectious disease risks in modern animal husbandry. Their regulatory restrictions due to antimicrobial resistance have placed an increasing need for antibiotic alternatives at the forefront of strategies to enhance food security (Gadde et al., 2017).

Antibiotic growth promoters are antibiotics that have been added to animal feeds at a sub-therapeutic dose to promote animal growth (Niewold, 2007). For about 50 years, AGPs have been used as a non-therapeutic dietary supplement to enhance production efficiency in livestock (Lillehoj et al., 2018; Oh et al., 2019). Moore et al., (1946) and Jukes et al., (1950) were the first publications that showed the beneficial effect of AGPs for poultry and swine (Dibner and Richards, 2005). These dietary antibiotics worked well in intensive animal production systems with enhanced production efficiencies and profits until researchers found potential human health threats from AGPs.

Prolonged and excessive usage of in-feed antibiotics led to the development of antimicrobial resistance genes among the microbiota, with negative implications becoming evident for human health (Kim and Ahn, 2022). The occurrence of antibiotic-resistance genes in the food chain was identified as a food safety concern by the United States Food and Drug Administration (FDA) in the early 1980s (Dibner and Richards, 2005). In 1986, Sweden took the very first step as a nation to ban AGPs in the animal industry (Dibner and Richards, 2005). Thereafter, many European Union (EU) countries limited or banned AGPs in the livestock industry. In the United States, in feed AGPs were eliminated as a consequence of consumer pressure for antibiotic-free animal production, although relatively fewer regulatory mandates were originally imposed compared to EU countries (Armbruster and Roberts, 2018).

With the imposed ban and consumer pressure on AGPs, food animal producers and scientists have been challenged to find solutions to sustain intensive production systems while maintaining the constant supply of meat and eggs. Correspondingly, research on possible alternatives for AGPs has emerged as a priority topic (Kurt et al., 2019). As discerned by Callaway et al. (2021), alternatives to antibiotics are “any substance that can serve as a substitute for therapeutic drugs that are increasingly become ineffective against pathogenic bacteria, viruses, or parasites”. Currently, a plethora of studies have reported promising alternatives for AGPs such as probiotics, prebiotics, synbiotics, botanicals, antimicrobial peptides, bacteriophages, and hyper-immune egg antibodies (Lee et al., 2022; Wickramasuriya et al., 2022). Most of these products are thought to mimic AGPs by maintaining or enhancing the growth performance, immunity, and health status of farm animals. One hypothesis is that alternatives to AGPs trigger growth performance by killing pathogenic bacteria (Niewold, 2007). Hence, it postulates that any chemical or biological substances that show antimicrobial activity may serve as a potential candidate for an alternative to AGPs. In contrast, some scientists argue that antibiotic alternatives for AGPs need to trigger natural metabolites associated with non-antibiotic anti-inflammatory effect on growth promotion and the overall health status of animals (Niewold, 2007). Nevertheless, the underlying mechanisms and the mode of action of many antibiotic alternative products have yet to be identified. Moreover, research outcomes from studies on antibiotic alternatives have been inconsistent when compared to studies on AGPs (Kurt et al., 2019).

In the last decade, a significant surge has been observed in the scientific literature on antibiotic alternative feed additives to promote gut health and production efficiency in farm animals (Lillehoj et al., 2018). Although many studies have been reported and reviewed, there is a need to identify research and publication trends on alternative AGPs for poultry to better understand gaps and research priorities (Kurt et al., 2019). In this review, we conducted a comprehensive bibliometric analysis to outline recent publication trends on alternatives to AGPs to inform researchers, funders of research, and policymakers on accomplishments to date and where gaps in research activities remain.

sec.title: Search Strategy
sec.p: The National Agricultural Library (NAL; Baltimore Ave, Beltsville, MD) developed a comprehensive search strategy for antibiotic alternatives and the alternatives categories with a primary focus on poultry sciences. Search results were limited to 2009-2022 to capture the recent activities related to the poultry science community more broadly related to antibiotics alternatives. Bibliometrics results are impacted by the content included in the specific research literature data used as the data source for the analysis. To minimize bias due to content selection practices of a single database, different bibliometric analysis data from Scopus, Web of Science/InCites, and Dimensions were used. This study was based on data obtained through public databases and did not involve any human subjects applicable for ethical consent.

sec.title: Selection of Search Terms
sec.p:
The search terms were developed using background articles and a scan of existing review literature on the topic. The search was organized into 3 concept areas:1.
Alternative to Antibiotics (including synonymous terms)2.
Specific alternative classes3.
Poultry

All identified antibiotic alternatives from background literature were categorized into 11 categories as shown in Figure 1. Specific search terms and strategies used for antibiotic alternatives are in Appendix 1. The term “poultry” was denoted to birds raised for meat, egg, and feathers in a commercial or domestic setup. Terms search strategies for poultry were limited to broiler chicken, layer hens, duck and goose, quails, and turkey.Figure 1
Eleven different categories that categorize all identified antibiotic alternatives from background literature.Figure 1

sec.title: Eligibility Screening and Study Characteristics
sec.p:
Authors from the NAL team performed the initial literature search and deduplication process. All the selected publications were deduplicated to avoid duplication of search results based on different databases. After deduplication, all the publications were imported into the Rayyan web application for screening using selected evidence-based review methods (Ouzzani et al., 2016). Each category of alternatives was thoroughly screened based on the title and abstract using decision options in Rayyan. We used the labeling option in Rayyan to further sort the publications into categories of alternatives. Non-peer-reviewed publications and publications in languages other than English were excluded. Original articles and review papers were included in this study after a thorough evaluation of the content and suitability to the search strings and category. Selected publications were checked between all authors to ensure whether these met the requirement for the inclusion criteria. The detailed inclusion and exclusion decisions are reported in a Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) diagram (Stovold et al., 2014) as shown in Figure 2.Figure 2
Detailed flow diagram of inclusion and exclusion process.Figure 2

sec.title: Data Extraction, Synthesis, and Visualization
sec.p: From the set of publications retrieved from Rayyan, data were manually extracted using a custom data template in Microsoft Excel. The count of published documents by type of alternative for poultry was reported as a percentage of the total. In addition, all publications pertaining to each type of alternative to antibiotic categories were listed by country and by publication year to make a count analysis based on publication year and country. To analyze institutional contributions, we categorized all publications into four categories: universities, government, research institutions, and industry/companies. Looking into species-based publications, we further divided all selected publications into specific poultry species. Further, we recognized the highly cited publications related to alternative to antibiotic classes for poultry published during 2009-2022 by screening citation reports from the Google Scholar citations as of May 25, 2023. Top journals publishing articles on alternatives to AGPs for poultry were synthesized and listed using the screened database in Microsoft Excel. All figures and tables were created using chart and graph options in Microsoft Excel software. A filled map option under chart type in Microsoft Excel software was used to visualize and compare the number of publications across geographical locations.

sec.title: Research Ethics
sec.p: All data were retrieved from public databases, and there were no human or animal subjects. Therefore, this bibliometric analysis does not require ethics approval.

sec.title: Publication by Class of Alternatives
sec.p: According to our search, a total of 2038 publications from Scopus, Web of Science, and Dimensions were eligible after initial screening. This included both the research and the review articles published during the search period from 2009 to 2022.

Among final screened documents (n = 816), the highest number of publications were published with probiotic accounting for 30.51% (n = 249) followed by phytogenic (24.02%, n = 196) and prebiotic (13.97%, n = 114) in all poultry species during search period (see Figure 3). All other categories accounted for less than 10% each. According to the data, heavy metals and clay represent the least number of publication categories for poultry during the search period, comprising only 1.47% (n = 12) of the total publications.Figure 3
Proportion of publications under eleven different categories that categorize all identified antibiotic alternatives for poultry.Figure 3

sec.title: Publication Growth Trend
sec.p:
Table 1 shows the number of publications produced each year related to 11 different alternatives to AGPs categories for poultry. Up until 2015, the total number of publications for poultry followed a slow and relatively stable curve with minor fluctuations. However, starting in 2015, there has been a noticeable upward global trend in alternatives to AGPs publications for poultry (Figure 4). In the majority of years, probiotics have been the most published alternative to the AGPs category for poultry. However, in 2018 and 2022, phytogenics emerged as the most published category.Table 1
Number of publications available for each of the alternatives to antibiotic classes for poultry.1Table 1
Publication year
Category20092010201120122013201420152016201720182019202020212022
Probiotics2357691014161239344448
Phytogenics04421436111921293557
Prebiotics464384458514171715
Organic Acids10521331453152111
Bacteriophages03306122222449
Synbiotics00102003233486
Enzymes00000001352764
Antimicrobial Peptides00011203163236
Novel Alternatives210110001233235
Vaccines10000000213355
Heavy Metals and Clays000001001102431
This study was based on bibliometric analysis data obtained through public databases (Scopus, Web of Science/InCites and Dimensions);2
Novel alternatives category compromised with all other alternatives to antibiotic growth promoters including metabolites, innate defense molecules, therapeutic antibodies, quorum-sensing inhibitor, and CRISPR-Cas.Figure 4
The top 5 publication categories and the total number of publications per year published for poultry.Figure 4

sec.title: Contribution of Countries
sec.p:
Among top publishing countries in the field of alternative to AGPs, The United States of America ranked as the number one publication country followed by China and Egypt during the 2009 to 2022 period (Figure 5). Looking into the number of papers published by countries per each alternative to AGPs category, the United States of America leads the most categories except synbiotics, heavy metals and clay, and enzymes (Table 2). As a country, Egypt leads in phytogenics, prebiotics, organic acids, synbiotics, and vaccine categories compared to China, although China published a greater number of papers as a total during the study period. China published more on probiotics, antimicrobial peptides, and novel alternatives following the United States of America. South Asian countries like India, Bangladesh, and Pakistan have made remarkable contributions to the field of alternatives to AGPs by publishing extensively on probiotics, organic acids, synbiotics, as well as heavy metals and clays.Figure 5
Top publishing countries for alternatives to antibiotic growth promoters for poultry from 2009 to 2022.Figure 5Table 2
Top 10 publishing countries for each of the alternatives to antibiotic classes for poultry.Table 2

sec.title: Publication by Poultry Species
sec.p:
Among poultry species, broilers are the most extensively researched group for alternatives to AGPs during this research period followed by Layer chickens (Table 3). However, many publications documented alternatives to AGPs' effect on 2 or more poultry species and made a broader conclusion describing them as “Chicken” or “Poultry.” Probiotics are the highest-researched category for chickens (broilers and layers), turkeys, ducks, and geese. Interestingly, phytogenics are the most documented category for quails. One publication was published with a prebiotic effect on Pigeon.Table 3
Number of publications available for each of the alternatives to antibiotic classes for different poultry species.Table 3
CategoryBroilerLayersChicken1TurkeysQuailsDuck and goosePigeonPoultry2
Probiotics14617248353046
Phytogenics13187182039
Prebiotics8621320121
Organic acids5700210015
Synbiotics250003103
Enzymes210000007
Bacteriophages1417001016
Vaccines130110006
Antimicrobial peptides1105000012
Novel alternatives111210005
Heavy metals and clays71100003
Total52230481617911731
studies pertaining chicken in a broader sense without focusing specifically either broiler or layers;2
studies describe either several species of poultry or general idea as poultry;3
One study was documented with Goose.

sec.title: Institutional Contributions to Publications
sec.p:
Based on the affiliations, all publications published during 2009 to 2022 were categorized into 4 categories: universities, government (including government research institutes), research institutes (non-governmental), and industry/company. Among the four categories, universities had the highest number of publications among other institutions (Figure 6). Following universities, government research institutes published the second largest number of publications for alternative AGPs for poultry. Probiotics are the most researched category by industry and universities, followed by phytogenics and prebiotics. Notably, there is a dearth of publications from industry/companies on vaccines, heavy metals and clay, and novel antibiotic alternatives for poultry.Figure 6
Distribution of institutions focusing on Alternatives to antibiotic growth promoters for poultry. (A) Percentage of publication produced by four different institutions on alternatives to antibiotic growth promoters for poultry; (B) Total number of publications produced by four different institutions (universities, government (including government research institutes), research institutes (non-governmental), and industry/company) on different alternatives to antibiotic growth promoter categories.Figure 6

sec.title: Journals Publishing on alternative AGPs for poultry
sec.p:
A total of 257 journals from different publishers published documents pertaining to alternatives to AGPs for poultry (data not shown). When the number of publications was taken into consideration, “Poultry Science (Elsevier)” published the highest amount with 13.51% publication records (Figure 7) followed by “Animals (MDPI)” which ranked second with 6.76% publications and “Journal of Applied Poultry Science (Elsevier)” placed third (3.69%), while “Frontiers in Veterinary Science (Frontiers)” placed fourth (2.58%). All other journals contributed less than 2% of total publications.Figure 7
Top 10 journals publishing articles on alternatives to antibiotic growth promoters for poultry.Figure 7

sec.title: Top Cited Publications on Alternative AGPs for Poultry
sec.p:
Based on the Google Scholar citation index, we sorted the most cited publications in the field of alternative AGPs for poultry published within the search period from 2009 to 2022 (Table 4). Among all publications, “Alternatives to antibiotics for maximizing growth performance and feed efficiency in poultry: a review” led by Dr Hyun Lillehoj from USDA-ARS ranked first in the number of citations (586 as of May 25, 2023), followed by “Effects of dietary polyphenol-rich grape products on intestinal microflora and gut morphology in broiler chicks” corresponded by Dr A. Brenes and “Antimicrobial peptides as potential alternatives to antibiotics in food animal industry” corresponded by Dr Shiyan Qiao. Looking into journals that published the most cited papers, it is evident that “Poultry Science” published the most highly cited publications accounting for 5 out of 11 highly cited articles.Table 4
Top 10 high-cited papers related to alternatives to antibiotic classes for poultry published during 2009 to 2022.1Table 4
ReferenceTitleCorresponding authorJournalTotal citations
Gadde et al., 2017Alternatives to antibiotics for maximizing growth performance and feed efficiency in poultry: a reviewHyun LillehojAnim. Health Res. Rev.586

Lillehoj et al., 2018Phytochemicals as antibiotic alternatives to promote growth and enhance host healthHyun LillehojVet. Res.305
Salim et al., 2013Supplementation of direct-fed microbials as an alternative to antibiotic on growth performance, immune response, cecal microbial population, and ileal morphology of broiler chickensW.K. KimPoult. Sci.274
Yang et al., 2012Effects of probiotic, Clostridium butyricum, on growth performance, immune function, and cecal microflora in broiler chickensA.G. ChenPoult. Sci.261

Diaz-Sanchez et al., 2015Botanical alternatives to antibiotics for use in organic poultry productionIrene HanningPoult. Sci.2101
Number of citation was derived from google citations as of 05.25.2023

sec.title: DISCUSSION
sec.p: The use of AGPs in the livestock industry is facing increased scrutiny due to alarming concerns about antibiotic resistance in food animals. The development of antibiotic resistance in bacteria poses not only significant risks to the health and production of poultry flocks, but antimicrobial resistance to medically important antibiotics can profoundly impact human health worldwide (Armbruster and Roberts, 2018). According to Consumer Reports, poultry products tested in US supermarkets showed resistance to antibiotics, with 57% of chicken and 83% of turkey being affected (Armbruster and Roberts, 2018). As a result, poultry producers are committing to raising poultry through antibiotic-free or no-antibiotics-ever farming practices as a responsible measure. To support this movement, researchers are continuously developing alternative strategies to replace AGPs in poultry. During the last few decades, a plethora of scientific publications, including research articles, reviews, and meta-analyses, were published pertaining to alternatives to AGPs for poultry. Nevertheless, a systematic review of alternatives to AGPs and publication trends was not found, and we believe this is the first systematic review of trend analysis in publications related to alternatives to AGPs.

In this study, we screened 2038 publications from three databases, as described previously. From those, 816 publications were selected for this systematic review after careful review of each abstract, experimental treatment, and study objectives. Despite the time-consuming nature of the task, we successfully assembled a suitable list of publications that aligns with our review objectives. Additionally, we carefully reviewed the content of each review article before including them in our analysis.

Among the various antibiotic alternatives to AGPs, results indicated that probiotics, phytogenics, and prebiotics emerge as the most extensively covered categories in the published literature (constituting over 65% of the total) for poultry, underscoring the popularity of these alternatives. While not subject to systematic review, previous reports have discussed the significance of probiotics and phytogenics, emphasizing their popularity as functional supplements when compared to other feed additives (Applegate et al., 2010; Jha et al., 2020). Based on previous literature, the prevailing consensus suggests that the administration of sub-therapeutic doses of antibiotics alters the composition of the intestinal microbiome to support gut health, ultimately leading to improved growth performance in poultry. Consequently, scientists are likely to channel their efforts toward enhancing poultry growth performance by promoting the cultivation of beneficial microflora in the intestinal tract. Moreover, a noteworthy point is that the majority of probiotics are classified as “Generally Recognized as Safe” and are considered viable alternatives to AGPs (Alayande et al., 2020). Consequently, more research is being conducted on prebiotics, probiotics, or direct-fed microbes as potential approaches from the beginning of the post-antibiotic-free era. On the other hand, researchers tend to look into different phytogenic products due to their antimicrobial, antioxidant, and antistress properties with the ability to modulate the intestinal microbiome (Al-Mnaser et al., 2022). Moreover, the availability of diverse forms of phytogenic products, such as plant extracts, plant powders, leafy materials, and oils, allows researchers to easily incorporate them into dietary treatments for poultry research studies (Lillehoj et al., 2018; Park et al., 2023). This accessibility simplifies the engagement of more scientists in this field, as they can readily explore the advantages and practical applications of phytogenics in poultry production. Although phytochemicals are widely adopted in the poultry industry as an alternative to AGPs, they have a historical reputation in Asian medicine for their distinctive medicinal properties (AlSheikh et al., 2020). Consequently, it's reasonable to posit that low- to mid-income Asian countries are more inclined to conduct and publish extensive phytogenic research due to the constraints of available funding. When compared to probiotics, prebiotics, and phytogenics, other alternatives like antimicrobial peptides, bacteriophages, vaccines, and novel approaches exhibit a lower number of publications in the context of poultry, each accounting for less than 5%. It can be inferred logically that the development and testing of these alternative categories, such as poultry vaccines, necessitate higher funding, substantial time, and specialized techniques and technology (Hoelzer et al., 2018). As a result, only a few research groups are dedicated to undertaking such projects involving poultry. Conversely, some of the publications related to enzymes, antimicrobial peptides, bacteriophages, and vaccines may be excluded during the selection process as they often lack a distinct focus on being alternatives to AGPs, or specific to poultry. In our previous correspondence, we identified that probiotics and phytogenics garner the greatest number of publications in poultry compared to other livestock species such as cattle, goats, and sheep (Wickramasuriya and Lillehoj, 2021). This suggests that the simple digestive system of poultry, along with their lower cost for field trials, makes them ideal for testing probiotic and phytogenic products, compared to ruminants and other expensive livestock.

As Hoelzer et al. (2018) discussed, vaccines, like other alternatives, must be safe, effective, cost-effective, and user-friendly. Failure to meet any of these criteria can reduce study interest and lead to fewer publications. Moreover, the lack of clear regulatory pathways may impede the pharmaceutical industry from investing in the research and development of antibiotic alternatives for poultry, likewise resulting in fewer clinical trials reported in the scientific literature. As such, the veterinary pharmaceutical industry considers that the regulation of veterinary medicines should evolve to accommodate the development of antibiotic alternatives, including incentives from regulatory agencies to further stimulate their development (Ioannou et al., 2018).

When examining the annual count of publications, a clear trend emerges, showcasing rapid growth in the cumulative number of publications focusing on alternatives to AGPs since 2015. Interestingly, the rate of publication increased from 2019 surpassed the period from 2015 to 2018. Pinpointing a single reason for the observed publication patterns over time is challenging; however, we believe these patterns result from the cumulative impact of various incidents and developments in the field. In 2013, the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) initiated a voluntary phase-out of medically important antibiotic labeling for animal growth promotion, and in 2015, it published the final rule of the Veterinary Feed Directive (VFD) (Lillehoj et al., 2018). Subsequently, the FDA mandated the removal of antibiotics for growth promotion starting in 2017 (Sneeringer et al., 2019). Additionally, China issued a directive in 2020 to cease the use of all AGPs except herbal medicines (Hu and Cowling, 2020). Furthermore, the indirect influence of the Covid-19 pandemic on publication trends is noteworthy. The pandemic has provided scientists with more time to analyze data and prepare manuscripts due to physical separation from research facilities.

In many scientific fields, it's a common occurrence to observe a substantial number of research publications originating from the United States of America and China. To discern the primary contributors by country in the realm of alternatives to AGPs, we conducted an analysis of the leading publishing countries within each category. During the data processing, we assigned equal credit to all countries for publications that involved two or more collaborations, aiming for a comprehensive and equitable evaluation. As expected, the United States of America and China emerged as the foremost producers of publications concerning alternatives to AGPs for poultry. Evidently, these two countries are investing considerable research efforts into the exploration of AGPs alternatives, which could be motivated by their strategies to curtail antimicrobial usage in livestock. Notably, an intriguing trend has emerged with an increase in publications from Egypt and South Asian countries in contrast to Australasian countries and Canada. This rising trend in publications might also align with Egypt's governmental approach to reducing antimicrobial usage in their agrifood system by up to 39-59% by 2030, in accordance with the “One Health” strategy, as reported during the Ministerial Conference on Antimicrobial Resistance in 2022 (Egypttoday, 2022). Furthermore, recent research on global trends in antimicrobial resistance in animals has shown the absence of major antimicrobial resistance in Africa compared to other high- and middle-income countries (Van Boeckel et al., 2019). This study has identified Asia as a significant hotspot for antimicrobial resistance (AMR) in livestock. Given these insights, it is challenging to definitively conclude that the rising publication trends in Egypt, Nigeria, and Asian countries directly correlate with the incidence of AMR. Additionally, data from Mulchandani et al. (2023) indicates that, although public reports are limited, Australia remains among the top five countries in antimicrobial usage. Consequently, it can be hypothesized that research on AGP alternatives from Australia (and potentially other Australasian countries) may not be adequately reflected in publication volume.

Based on our observations in this analysis, it becomes evident that a significant portion of alternative AGP research focuses on broiler studies, followed by studies involving layer chickens. In certain publications, a combination or general reference is made to “chicken” without specifying whether the strains are broiler or layer chickens. Furthermore, some publications discuss one or more poultry species under the umbrella term “poultry.” In most cases, review articles encompass poultry as a broader category. Consequently, we have categorized these publications separately, either under “Chicken” or “Poultry”. The dominance of broiler studies in terms of publication count is not surprising. Broilers constitute a major poultry subsector with the highest production levels and strong global demand for antibiotic-free products. On closer examination, the relatively shorter study period and lower costs associated with broiler studies compared to layer studies may also contribute to the higher publication rate within the broiler category. Moreover, it is also likely that the scale of research on broilers reflects the relative scale of feed production for broilers and the corresponding funding from granting agencies and companies involved in broiler production. In contrast, fewer publications were recorded for turkey and quail studies. This could be attributed to the relatively fewer research groups worldwide working on turkey and quail studies. Moreover, the overall lower utilization (as per market segment) of AGPs for growth enhancement and disease control in these species compared to broilers may result in reduced interest in developing alternative solutions. Analyzing the geographical distribution of turkey and quail studies reveals that the majority of turkey research is concentrated in the USA, while quail studies are predominantly conducted in Iran and Egypt (data not presented). This distribution reflects the popularity of these birds in different regions. Quail meat and eggs, recognized for their higher protein content and health benefits, enjoy popularity in the Middle East and Asian markets (Lukanov, 2019). Conversely, North American countries have a stronger tradition-driven demand for turkey, particularly during occasions like Thanksgiving. In our dataset, it's evident that studies involving probiotics are more prevalent in relation to turkeys, whereas a higher number of phytogenic studies are reported for quails. The elevated number of phytogenic studies involving quails might be linked to the geographical distribution patterns discussed earlier in relation to phytogenic research.

By categorizing publications based on affiliated institutions, we have aimed to delineate the contributions of various entities to alternatives to AGPs research. Our findings reveal that universities have made the most significant contributions, followed by government agencies and industries. It's noteworthy that the majority of these publications are attributed to doctoral students and postdoctoral researchers affiliated with universities. While the number of publications stemming from government institutes may be lower, it is well-known that many research projects receive funding from government research funds (National Science Board, 2021). When considering the institutional contributions within each alternative category, it is apparent that industries and companies exhibit a higher interest in probiotics compared to government institutes, despite government institutes leading in other alternative categories. This trend could be attributed to the burgeoning demand for probiotics in animal feed and the potential future market opportunities that drive industrial interest in probiotic research. Furthermore, it's pertinent to delve into the reasons behind the comparatively lower number of publications from industries in areas such as vaccines, heavy metals, clay, and novel alternatives. This discrepancy might underscore the significant gap in industrial involvement for these particular alternative categories when compared to the interest shown in probiotic and phytogenic alternatives. Companies often test new additives in commercial-scale trials using their typical diets and environmental conditions. If these additives do not promote growth, improve feed efficiency, or reduce mortality, they are rejected without further consideration. As a result, commercial trials focus on pragmatic and economically viable performance metrics. In contrast, university trials emphasize non-commercial but scientifically interesting indices such as intestinal health, immune status, and microbial diversity. Therefore, It's conceivable that companies prioritize major alternatives like probiotics due to a potentially higher return on investment, as opposed to the resource-intensive and less successful nature of projects involving vaccines and novel alternatives, which are also hampered by more complex regulatory processes (Hoelzer et al., 2018).

sec.title: Strengths and Limitations
sec.p: The data presented in this review is both relatively objective and comprehensive, derived from extracted information from Scopus, Web of Science, and Dimensions databases, all managed in an impartial manner. However, certain limitations are inevitable. Since our inclusion criteria were restricted to English-language publications, there is a possibility that some crucial research articles concerning alternatives to AGPs, published in non-English journals, might have been excluded from both the database and subsequent analysis. Additionally, due to the specific focus of our inclusion criteria, some studies that involve different feed additives could have been excluded if they didn't explicitly indicate an interest in alternatives to AGPs in their study objectives or if they didn't incorporate the key search terms.

It's important to acknowledge that older but significant research papers published before 2009 were omitted from this review due to our primary emphasis on recent publications. Moreover, in some instances, it was challenging to isolate discussions about alternatives to antibiotics for poultry from review articles that discussed livestock or monogastric animals as a whole. However, a noteworthy strength of our approach is that we meticulously read every individual abstract during the screening process, enabling a more precise and accurate selection process. This diligence contributes to the reliability of the data collected and analyzed.

sec.title: CONCLUSIONS
sec.p: This study was conducted to systematically analyze recent publication trends regarding diverse antibiotic alternatives to AGPs for poultry. Our bibliometric analysis indicates that probiotics, prebiotics, and phytogenics are the leading antibiotic alternatives for promoting poultry growth. Furthermore, we can project a promising market trajectory for these categories. Notably, the amalgamation of multiple alternatives into a single product is poised to shape the industry's future, aiming for improved efficacy similar to AGPs. Enhanced legislative and funding support is imperative to bolster research efforts in the domains of vaccines, antimicrobial peptides, and other innovative alternatives. The highest publication trends by the USA and China reported in this study may remain the same in the future while more research and publications may be available from Asian countries. Further antibiotic alternative research on other poultry categories such as turkey is needed to support antibiotic-free products. In addition, bibliometric analysis with other livestock species may be recommended in future studies to broaden the understanding of research trends and markets.

sec.title: ACKNOWLEDGMENTS
sec.p: This review paper was funded by ARS CRIS project number 8042-32000-115-00D.
Availability of Data and Materials: The datasets generated and analyzed in this study are available from the corresponding author on reasonable request.

Authors’ Contributions: HSL and CGG conceptualized, supervised, and edited the manuscript. JA extracted all data and performed the bibliometric analyses. SR supervised the data extraction and bibliometric analyses. SSW and JA undertook and refined the searches. SSW performed the data visualization and drafted the manuscript. All authors contributed to and revised the final manuscript.
Disclaimer: The views and opinions of the authors expressed herein do not necessarily state or reflect those of the National Agriculture Library, or the US Department of Agriculture.

sec.title: DISCLOSURES
sec.p: The authors declare no conflicts of interest.

fn.p: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.psj.2024.103987.
